title: Counts
subtitle: Type a number before any motion or operator. It multiplies.
summary: Most Vim commands accept a count prefix. 5j moves down 5 lines. 3dw deletes 3 words. Counts on both sides of an operator multiply.

blocks.text: Almost every Vim command accepts a numeric prefix. The number means "do this N times." That's it — no escape sequence, no special key. Just type the digits, then the command.

blocks.title: Counts Multiply
blocks.text:
When a count appears on *both* sides of an operator, the two are **multiplied**:

  • {key:2}{key:d}{key:3}{key:w} = delete 6 words.
  • {key:3}{key:y}{key:2}{key:y} = yank 6 lines.
  • {key:5}{key:>}{key:2}{key:j} = indent 15 lines (current + 14 below).

This is consistent with the grammar: {operator-count} × {motion-count}. Most of the time you'll only use one of them; both is rare but legal.

Note that the count goes *between* the register and the operator if you also use a register: {key:"}{key:a}{key:3}{key:d}{key:w} — "into register a, three times, delete word."

Counts work in Insert mode too. {key:2}{key:0}{key:i}-{key:Esc} inserts twenty dashes. The count is replayed when you leave Insert. (Some non-character Insert commands like Tab don't behave well with this — try it before relying on it.)
{key:Ctrl-A} and {key:Ctrl-X} use counts to add/subtract from the next number on the line — see [`recipes.increment-decrement`](#recipes.increment-decrement).

rows: Apply op (n × m) times over motion
Repeat last change with new count n
